title: Wavelet-based Recovery Design
text:
WARA uses wavelet packet decomposition (WPD) to separate acoustic attack interference from benign sensor signals. Unlike classical wavelet transforms that only decompose low-frequency components, WPD decomposes both low- and high-frequency bands recursively, producing a full binary tree of sub-bands. This allows WARA to isolate attack interference localized in specific frequency bands while preserving the drone’s motion signal.



3-Level Wavelet Packet Decomposition
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Impact of External Disturbance
text: External disturbances such as wind gusts affect drone state estimation in various ways. However, these disturbances typically induce consistent motion across all redundant IMUs on the flight controller board. This consistency supports WARA’s approach: redundant IMUs experience the same benign disturbances, allowing the detectors to distinguish between external forces (common to all IMUs) and acoustic attack interference (isolated to specific IMUs).
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
text: We designed a low-cost ($28) acoustic injection attack device capable of simultaneously targeting multiple heterogeneous IMUs (Son et al. 2015; Trippel et al. 2017; Tu et al. 2018; Jeong et al. 2023) on a drone — including both gyroscopes and accelerometers. To our knowledge, this is the first work to report simultaneous acoustic injection attacks at different frequencies against multiple IMUs. The compact unit (\(9 \times 7\ \text{cm}^2\)) supports two complementary attack modes: remote attacks via ultrasonic emitters, and touch-based attacks via piezoelectric transducer (PZT) discs mounted directly on the flight controller.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV
text:
Yunbo Wang · Cong Sun✉ · Qiaosen Liu
Xidian University · Xi’an, Shaanxi, China
✉ Corresponding author: suncong@xidian.edu.cn
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: WARA
text:
Yunbo Wang · Xidian University · robertwang@stu.xidian.edu.cn
Cong Sun · Xidian University · suncong@xidian.edu.cn
Qiaosen Liu · Xidian University · liuqiaosen666@163.com
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV
text:
Abstract
Acoustic injection attacks seriously threaten the operation of micro-electromechanical system (MEMS) inertial sensors, including accelerometers and gyroscopes, and thus can crash the drone whose flight control inevitably relies on these sensors. The state-of-the-art attack mitigations have limited effectiveness and high runtime overheads, and few have been validated against real-world acoustic injection attacks, particularly the simultaneous attacks on heterogeneous IMUs. We demonstrated that such multi-IMU acoustic injections can be effectively implemented remotely and can overwhelm common hardware protections, e.g., physical shielding and IMU redundancy, on commercial flight controller boards. We present WARA, an effective and low-overhead acoustic attack mitigation technique that recovers benign sensor signals from compromised MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers. WARA extracts and removes acoustic attack interference with the wavelet packet decomposition. With a redundant IMU instance, WARA can discern low-frequency attack interference from benign signals, even if the redundant instance is also compromised. The WARA implementation on PX4 validates its real-time capability on commercial hardware (CUAV V5+ and Pixhawk 6C Mini). Our evaluations confirm that WARA achieves longer recovery durations than the SOTA PBAR, the deep-learning-based denoising method, and specification-aware recovery. Real-world flight tests demonstrate that WARA affords stable flight even under the acoustic injection attacks.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Detector Parameter Selection
text:
WARA uses CUSUM-based detectors on each wavelet packet to identify attack-affected signal components. The detectors are configured with two parameters: a mean shift (\(b_j\)) that suppresses normal measurement fluctuations, and a detection threshold (\(\tau\)) that triggers an alarm when the cumulative statistic exceeds it.
Selecting appropriate parameters is critical: too sensitive and normal flight maneuvers trigger false alarms; too conservative and real attacks go undetected. WARA selects parameters based on time to detection (TTD) and false positive rate during attack-free flights.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
To compare all approaches on a common metric, we use effective recovery duration: the elapsed time from the first detection alarm (\(t_{alarm}\)) until the drone’s estimated position deviates from the actual position by more than \(\epsilon = 3\) meters — indicating loss of stable flight. This metric reflects sustained operational safety under attack, not merely signal-level recovery quality. All results below are measured in HITL simulation on the same hardware for fair comparison.



Effective recovery duration across all baselines (HITL, \(G_i\) and \(f_i\) as labeled)


WARA achieves the longest effective recovery durations across all tested attack configurations. UnRocker’s high processing delay prevents timely recovery even when its signal-level denoising is effective. VIMU’s physical model degrades under strong attacks and sampling jitter. SpecGuard’s RL controller cannot compensate when all IMU instances are simultaneously compromised. The classical filters — Butterworth, Savitzky-Golay, and Wiener — either fail to remove low-frequency interference or introduce motion signal distortion that shortens recovery duration. The following sections examine each comparison in detail.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV
text:
Overview

Problem — Acoustic injection attacks can crash drones by manipulating MEMS inertial sensors (gyroscopes & accelerometers). Existing mitigations have limited effectiveness and high runtime overhead.
Attack — We build a low-cost ($28) attack device capable of simultaneously injecting acoustic interference into multiple heterogeneous IMUs, bypassing hardware protections like physical shielding and IMU redundancy.
Solution — WARA decomposes sensor signals with wavelet packet transform, isolates attack interference by amplitude, and reconstructs benign signals using a redundant IMU instance — even if that instance is also compromised.
Implementation — Real-time on PX4 autopilot, tested on CUAV V5+ and Pixhawk 6C Mini flight controllers.
Results — Longer recovery duration than SOTA (PBAR, deep-learning denoising, specification-aware recovery). Sustained stable flight for 2–5 minutes under real-world acoustic attacks.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV
text:
Key Contributions


🎯 Multi-IMU Attacks
We develop a low-cost ($28) attack device that simultaneously injects acoustic interference into multiple heterogeneous IMUs, bypassing physical shielding and IMU redundancy on commercial flight controllers.


🔬 Wavelet Recovery
WARA uses wavelet packet decomposition to isolate attack interference by amplitude. A redundant IMU instance preserves benign signal components — even if all IMUs are compromised.


🚁 Real-World Flights
Implemented on PX4 autopilot (CUAV V5+, Pixhawk 6C Mini). Real-world flight tests sustain stable flight for 2–5 minutes under acoustic attacks, far exceeding SOTA recovery durations.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
text: We designed a low-cost ($28) acoustic injection attack device capable of simultaneously targeting multiple heterogeneous IMUs (Son et al. 2015; Trippel et al. 2017; Tu et al. 2018; Jeong et al. 2023) on a drone — including both gyroscopes and accelerometers. To our knowledge, this is the first work to report simultaneous acoustic injection attacks at different frequencies against multiple IMUs. The compact unit (\(9 \times 7\ \text{cm}^2\)) supports two complementary attack modes: remote attacks via ultrasonic emitters, and touch-based attacks via piezoelectric transducer (PZT) discs mounted directly on the flight controller.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
text:
Remote Attack Deployment
The device supports two attack modes via different transducer types: ultrasonic emitters for remote attacks and PZT discs for touch-based attacks (deployed directly on the drone).
For remote attacks, we deploy dedicated \(2 \times 2\) emitter arrays for each target IMU. Each array emits acoustic signals tuned to the resonance frequency band of its target IMU, maximizing impact. Multiple arrays can operate from the same orientation.



Attack orientation relative to the flight controller (top-down view)




Multi-IMU Remote Attack Demonstration (Lab)



F330 Remote Attack → Crash (Real-World, 0.20 rad/s)



F330 Dual-IMU Attack on Vanilla ArduPilot (No WARA)
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
text:
Attack Device Hardware
The entire attack unit is assembled from off-the-shelf components and costs merely $28, fitting within a \(9 \times 7\ \text{cm}^2\) footprint — small enough to be carried by a quadcopter for in-flight attacks.

Components







Component
Role




Raspberry Pi Zero 2W
Microcontroller — generates PWM control signals


L298N
Motor driver module — drives multiple transducers


MT3608 DC-DC boost converter
Steps 5V up to 20V to maximize attack amplitude


5V/2A power bank
Powers the entire unit


Ultrasonic emitters
Remote attacks (air-coupled)


PZT discs
Touch-based attacks (direct contact, stronger signal)



The microcontroller adjusts the pulse-width and frequency of each PWM channel to independently control the amplitude and frequency of the acoustic signal delivered to each transducer.


Wiring Diagram



Wiring diagram of the attack device




Transducer Layout on Flight Controllers
The transducers are positioned directly above each target IMU on the flight controller board. IMU locations can be determined from public board schematics or through trial-and-error on an identical board.



Transducer layout on CUAV V5+





Transducer layout on Pixhawk 6C Mini





Attack device mounted on F330 quadcopter
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
text:
Hardware



Wiring diagram of the attack device


The attack device uses a Raspberry Pi Zero 2W as the microcontroller, which controls an L298N motor driver module to generate multiple PWM signals. Compared to prior work that relies on a digital-to-analog converter and power amplifier chain (Gao et al. 2024), we introduce a DC-DC boost converter (MT3608, stepping 5V up to 20V) to drive the transducers more efficiently. A mini power bank (5V/2A) powers the entire unit.
The device supports two transducer types, each serving a distinct role:

Ultrasonic emitters — for remote attacks at a distance. These are the core attack scenario, enabling multi-IMU simultaneous injection without physical contact.
PZT discs — for touch-based attacks attached directly to the flight controller. These achieve stronger amplitudes and can penetrate the physical shielding found on commercial IMUs. For instance, the CUAV V5+ board encases its IMU sensors in a thick metal block that effectively reduces remote attack amplitudes. Touch-based PZT attacks significantly weaken this protection, allowing us to evaluate WARA’s recovery robustness across a broader range of IMU models.

By adjusting pulse-width and frequency, the microcontroller independently controls the amplitude and frequency of the acoustic signal on each PWM channel. Generally, this device achieves remote attack amplitudes ranging from 0.2 to 2.0 rad/s on each targeted IMU — broader coverage than prior work (Jeong et al. 2023; Gao et al. 2024).
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
text:
Remote Attack in Lab Setting
We demonstrated remote multi-IMU attacks in a laboratory setting. Commercial flight controllers typically equip multiple heterogeneous IMUs for redundancy, and each IMU model has a distinct resonance frequency band. Because these bands rarely overlap, an attacker cannot compromise all IMUs with a single acoustic frequency — hence the need for simultaneous multi-frequency injection.
For each target IMU, we assemble a dedicated \(2 \times 2\) ultrasonic emitter array placed 25 cm from the flight controller. Each array emits acoustic signals tuned to the resonance frequency band of its target IMU. During evaluation, the drone motors were armed to assess the impact of motor noise on attack effectiveness.


Multi-IMU Remote Attack Demonstration (Lab)
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
section: From Remote Emitter Arrays to Single PZT Transducer
text:
From Remote Emitter Arrays to Single PZT Transducer
While remote emitter arrays demonstrate the feasibility of multi-IMU attacks, deploying them on a flying drone is impractical due to their size and distance requirements. We found that a single PZT transducer attached directly to the flight controller can achieve similar or stronger attack amplitudes at much closer range (1 cm). This enables a compact, in-flight attack unit deployable on the drone itself.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

Transducers are positioned directly above each target IMU. IMU locations are determined from public board schematics or through trial-and-error on an identical board.

title: Attack Device Specification
text:
Real-World Flight Tests
The attack device was deployed on an F330 quadcopter for real-world flight testing. Even at low amplitudes (~0.20 rad/s), remote attacks can cause an unprotected drone to crash.


F330 Remote Attack → Crash (Real-World, 0.20 rad/s)



F330 Dual-IMU Attack on Vanilla ArduPilot (No WARA)
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Attack Device Specification
section: From Emitter Arrays to Closer-Range Attacks
text:
From Emitter Arrays to Closer-Range Attacks
Interestingly, we observed that a single ultrasonic emitter at 7 cm can achieve similar attack amplitudes as a full \(2 \times 2\) array at 25 cm. Inspired by this, we developed closer-range attacks at just 1 cm to further increase attack intensity. This compact setup — a single emitter placed near the IMU — can be deployed directly on the drone for real-world flight tests, achieving attack amplitudes up to ~2.2 rad/s on the BMI055 IMU.
Although our prototype is limited to short range, long-range attacks remain viable with high-power hardware (e.g., a Genasys LRAD 1950XL (Genasys 2025) could achieve equivalent intensity at over 12 meters (Son et al. 2015)).
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Wavelet-based Recovery Design
text:
WARA uses wavelet packet decomposition (WPD) to separate acoustic attack interference from benign sensor signals. Unlike classical wavelet transforms that only decompose low-frequency components, WPD decomposes both low- and high-frequency bands recursively, producing a full binary tree of sub-bands. This allows WARA to isolate attack interference localized in specific frequency bands while preserving the drone’s motion signal.



3-Level Wavelet Packet Decomposition
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Wavelet-based Recovery Design
text:
The Mallat Algorithm
WPD is implemented via the Mallat algorithm, which performs fast level-wise transformations using a pair of low-pass (\(h[\cdot]\)) and high-pass (\(g[\cdot]\)) filter banks. Given an input sequence \(x[\cdot]\) of length \(N\) and filter length \(F\), the decomposition produces two sequences of wavelet coefficients:
\[w_{a}[k] = \sum^F_{i=0}x[2k-i]h[i];\quad w_{d}[k] = \sum^F_{i=0}x[2k-i]g[i]\]
where \(k=0,1,...,\frac{N}{2}-1\), and \(w_{a}[k]\) and \(w_{d}[k]\) are the approximate and detail wavelet coefficients. The decomposition propagates recursively: approximate coefficients go to the left child and detail coefficients go to the right child in the WPT tree.
Reconstruction reverses the process using synthesis filters \(h^*[\cdot]\) and \(g^*[\cdot]\):
\[y[2k] = \sum^{F/2}_{i=0} (w_{d}[k{-}i]g^*[2i] + w_{a}[k{-}i]h^*[2i])\]
\[y[2k{+}1] = \sum^{F/2}_{i=0}(w_{d}[k{-}i]g^*[2i{+}1] + w_{a}[k{-}i]h^*[2i{+}1])\]
When the filter banks are orthogonal (\(g[\cdot]\cdot g^*[\cdot]=h[\cdot]\cdot h^*[\cdot]=1/F\)), the decomposition and reconstruction form an invertible linear transformation — WPD preserves the energy and details of the input signal. If \(w_{a}[k]\) or \(w_d[k]\) is missing, the corresponding term in the reconstruction is simply zero.
Decomposing to level-\(L\) requires the input length to be an exact multiple of \(2^L\). When the input is too short, we apply reflect padding at the tail to ensure continuity and alleviate edge distortion in the reconstructed signal.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Wavelet-based Recovery Design
text:
Online WPD with Buffer Mechanism

Motivation
A naive WPD implementation stores all historical wavelet coefficients at every tree level, causing exponential space growth with tree depth — far exceeding the memory of typical FC boards. Processing all historical coefficients also exceeds their computational capacity. WARA addresses both problems with a buffer mechanism that reuses past computation results.


Buffer Size Derivation
WARA’s goal is to reconstruct only the latest induced signal estimate \(\hat{A}_P(t)\) to compute the recovered measurement. According to the Mallat reconstruction equations, reconstructing at level-0 requires each level-1 node to provide at least \(F/2\) wavelet coefficients. Let \(m(L)\) be the number of coefficients each level-\(L\) node must provide to level \(L-1\):
\[m(L)=\left\{
\begin{array}{ll}
\frac{F}{2} & L=1 \\
\frac{F}{2} + \lceil \frac{m(L-1)-2}{2} \rceil & L\ge 2
\end{array}
\right.\]
The term \(\frac{F}{2}\) accounts for reconstructing the last two outputs; \(\lceil \frac{m(L)-2}{2} \rceil\) covers the remaining outputs. Since \(\lceil \frac{m(L)-2}{2} \rceil < \frac{m(L)}{2}\), we can bound this by a simpler recurrence \(n(L)\):
\[m(L) < n(L) = F\cdot(1-\frac{1}{2^L}) < F;\quad L\geq 1\]
Therefore, storing the last \(F\) wavelet coefficients at each node is sufficient for flawless reconstruction of \(\hat{A}_P(t)\). To keep these \(F\) coefficients up to date, the decomposition requires \(3F\) inputs. Reflect padding provides the last \(F\) inputs, reducing the actual input length to \(2F\).


Buffer Design
Each WPT node maintains two buffers: - Decomposition buffer (length \(2F\)) — stores recent sensor samples or parent coefficients for decomposition - Reconstruction buffer (length \(F+2\)) — stores output coefficients for reconstruction (the extra 2 slots ensure proper data alignment)
Each node also tracks a starting index \(i_{start}(t)\), initialized based on the node’s level \(L\) and filter length \(F\):
\[i_{start}(0) = ((F-1) \gg L) \mathbin{\&} 1\]
where \(\gg\) is bit-wise right-shift and \(\&\) is bit-wise AND. When a new input enters the decomposition buffer, the index updates: \(i_{start}(t) = (i_{start}(t-1)+1) \bmod 2\). When two new inputs arrive at a level-\(L\) node, the decomposition buffer of its level-\((L+1)\) child shifts backward by one element to store the new wavelet coefficient. The starting index is then used to slice the correct subsequence from each buffer for wavelet decomposition and reconstruction — ensuring the online implementation remains consistent with the Mallat algorithm.


Three Procedures
WARA’s online WPD pipeline is driven by three procedures. Buffers shift forward as new sensor data arrives, and shift flags propagate from root to leaves, synchronizing computations across tree levels.
1. Buffer Management (ManageBuffer)
Each node’s shift flag is determined by its parent: a child shifts its buffer only when the parent’s buffer has shifted and the parent’s starting index equals 1 (indicating two new inputs have been accumulated). If no shift is needed, the node is skipped. When a shift occurs, the buffer slides backward by one position, discarding the oldest sample.
2. Online Decomposition
For each new sensor sample \(\tilde{x}(t)\): 1. Shift the root node buffer and insert \(\tilde{x}(t)\) at the end 2. Traverse the tree depth-first. For each non-root node: - Manage buffer shift (propagated from parent) - Slice the parent’s input buffer starting at \(i_{start}(t)\) - Apply the Mallat decomposition (Eq.1) to produce \(w[k]\) - Store the coefficients in the current node’s buffer
3. Online Reconstruction with Recovery
For each new sample from the primary IMU: 1. Traverse the tree depth-first. At each leaf node: - If the node is anomalous and LF: subtract the corresponding coefficient from a redundant IMU instance, cancelling out benign motion while preserving the attack residual - If the node is anomalous and HF: zero out the coefficients (attack-only band) - If the node is attack-free: preserve coefficients as-is 2. When both children of a parent node are ready, reconstruct the parent’s output via the Mallat synthesis equations (Eqs.2–3), propagating upward 3. Read the recovered attack estimate \(\hat{A}_P(t)\) from the root node’s reconstruction buffer 4. Return \(\hat{x}_P(t) = \tilde{x}_P(t) - \hat{A}_P(t)\) — the recovered benign measurement
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Wavelet-based Recovery Design
text:
WPT Partition
The partition of wavelet packets determines which frequency bands are monitored for attack interference. We use the Symlets 2 wavelet (\(F=4\)) and configure the WPT based on the drone’s motion signal characteristics.



Wavelet Packet Configuration (Single-IMU and Multi-IMU modes)



Frequency Band Allocation









Band


Range


Depth


Purpose




LF (Low Frequency)


0–20 Hz


Level 6


Captures drone motion signals (0–5 Hz) plus low-frequency attack interference near the motion band. Decomposed deeper for fine-grained isolation.




HF (High Frequency)


20–100 Hz


Level 4


Captures attack interference above the motion band. Shallower depth saves computation.




Rejected


>100 Hz


—


Removed by the autopilot’s hardware low-pass filter; not processed.



The LF band is slightly broader than the motion signal band (0–5 Hz) to accurately capture attack interference that falls within or near the motion signal frequencies — the most critical type of interference for flight stability.


Single-IMU vs. Multi-IMU Mode
The assignment of level-6 wavelet packets depends on whether a redundant IMU instance is available:
Single-IMU mode: Wavelet packets \(w_{aaaddd}\) through \(w_{aaadad}\) are designated as HF attack packets and zeroed out during recovery. The remaining level-6 packets are preserved as-is to retain the motion signal. This conservative approach avoids distorting benign data but cannot remove low-frequency attack interference.
Multi-IMU mode: All level-6 wavelet packets are treated as LF attack packets and replaced with the corresponding coefficients from a redundant (reserved) IMU instance. Since the reserved IMU is unlikely to experience the same attack interference in the exact same time-frequency bins, its coefficients provide a clean substitute — enabling removal of even low-frequency interference without distorting the motion signal.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Detector Parameter Selection
text:
WARA uses CUSUM-based detectors on each wavelet packet to identify attack-affected signal components. The detectors are configured with two parameters: a mean shift (\(b_j\)) that suppresses normal measurement fluctuations, and a detection threshold (\(\tau\)) that triggers an alarm when the cumulative statistic exceeds it.
Selecting appropriate parameters is critical: too sensitive and normal flight maneuvers trigger false alarms; too conservative and real attacks go undetected. WARA selects parameters based on time to detection (TTD) and false positive rate during attack-free flights.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Detector Parameter Selection
text:
Parameter Selection Procedure

Data Collection
We use a specially designed maneuver mission to gather attack-free flight data. This mission spans typical flight scenarios — takeoff, hover, cruise, and turning — and includes several successive sharp turns to stress-test flight performance. The diversity of conditions and emphasis on drastic maneuvers make this mission ideal for parameter selection, as detector behavior in attack-free flight is primarily affected by measurement noise and environmental disturbances.
We collect 10 attack-free flight records in both SITL and HITL simulations, with fault isolation and recovery functionality disabled to ensure mission completion. For each wavelet packet, we extract the wavelet coefficients and residuals (\(\bar{r}_j(t)\) and \(r^{\prime}_j(t)\)) to construct a validation dataset.


Determining Candidate Mean Shifts
From the validation dataset, we derive candidate mean shifts for each wavelet packet:

LF packets (Trend): Average amplitude of \(\bar{r}_j(t)\) → candidate \(\bar{b}_{P,j}\)
LF packets (Transient): Average amplitude of \(r^{\prime}_j(t)\) → candidate \(b^{\prime}_{P,j}\)
HF packets: Average amplitude of \(|w_{P,j}(t)|\) for each HF packet; the smallest average is chosen as \(b_{P,j}\) (representing the background noise level)

The cutoff frequency for trend features is set to \(f_{CUTOFF} = 10\) Hz, consistent with the motion signal band.


Threshold Selection (4 Steps)

Find minimum thresholds — For each candidate mean shift, use the validation dataset from each flight to identify the minimum threshold \(\tau_{min}\) that avoids false alarms in that flight.
Determine reference threshold — Choose the largest \(\tau_{min}\) across all 10 flights as the reference threshold \(\tau_{ref}\). A detector configured with \(\tau_{ref}\) guarantees zero false positives across all 10 missions.
Evaluate and confirm — Configure detectors with different candidate mean shifts and their corresponding \(\tau_{ref}\) values. Evaluate TTD at various attack deviations, where \(TTD = t_{alarm} - t_{atk}\) (the elapsed time from attack onset to first alarm). Select the mean shift that provides the best trade-off between detection speed and false positive avoidance.
Apply safety margin — Add a 5% safety margin to the selected \(\tau_{ref}\): \[\tau = 1.05 \cdot \tau_{ref}\]
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Detector Parameter Selection
text:
Simulation Detector Parameters
Following the procedure above, the table below presents the detector parameters used in SITL and HITL experiments. A detection threshold of \(\tau = 3.0\) is used uniformly across all wavelet packets.

crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Impact of External Disturbance
text: External disturbances such as wind gusts affect drone state estimation in various ways. However, these disturbances typically induce consistent motion across all redundant IMUs on the flight controller board. This consistency supports WARA’s approach: redundant IMUs experience the same benign disturbances, allowing the detectors to distinguish between external forces (common to all IMUs) and acoustic attack interference (isolated to specific IMUs).
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Impact of External Disturbance
text:
Wind Robustness of Detector Parameters

Simulation Setup
We use Gazebo to simulate wind at different intensities. Gazebo models wind as a velocity vector drawn from a normal distribution: total wind speed \(v_{wind} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_w, \sigma_w^2)\) and direction \(\mathbf{w}_d \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_d, \sigma_d^2)\). The default configuration uses \(\mu_w = 4.0\) m/s with no variance. We set \(\sigma_w = 6.0\) m/s to simulate the disturbance in wind gust, and we injected east-axis wind with \(\mu_w' = 4.0/10.0/15.0\) m/s for wind speeds of 4 m/s, 10 m/s, and 15 m/s.


Parameter Generalization
We test whether detector parameters selected under default wind conditions generalize to stronger winds. The figures below show \(\tau_{min}\) (minimum threshold for zero false alarms) across different mean shifts and wind settings.



τ_min curves for gyroscope detectors in the wavelet-based recovery module under different wind settings





τ_min curves for detectors in the fault isolation module under different wind settings


In both detector modules, the optimal detection thresholds exceed all \(\tau_{min}\) values even at stronger wind speeds, indicating a 0% false positive rate across all wind conditions. The selected parameters generalize well — no recalibration is needed when wind intensity changes.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Impact of External Disturbance
text:
Detection and Recovery Under Wind
We further examine how wind affects time to detection and recovery duration during an active acoustic attack (\(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 20\) Hz). The detector successfully identifies the attack in all flights regardless of wind speed.



Time to detection at different wind speeds. Vertical bars show average TTD


The TTD remains stable across wind speeds, confirming that external disturbances do not significantly delay attack detection.
The average effective recovery duration stays above 300 seconds as wind speeds rise from 4.0 m/s to 10.0 m/s (330.5 s vs. 319.4 s across 10 flights). At 15.0 m/s, the drone failed to hold position against strong winds, reducing the recovery duration to 22.0 s. Still, WARA maintained flight attitude and avoided the imminent crash from the acoustic attack.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Impact of External Disturbance
text:
Other Noise Sources
Beyond wind, sensor measurements are also affected by mechanical vibration, electrical noise, and other persistent sources. Because these noise sources remain stable over the long term and are already present in attack-free flight data, they are accounted for in the mean shift \(b_j\). WARA detects attacks by identifying amplitudes that exceed the baseline established during attack-free flight, so these persistent noise sources do not significantly impact detection performance.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Comparison with UnRocker
text:
We first compare WARA’s recovery performance with UnRocker, the SOTA deep-learning-based denoising approach, in HITL simulation. Both methods run on a Raspberry Pi 3B+, which is the only board in our test suite with sufficient memory (1 GB) to load UnRocker’s autoencoder model (1.43 MB).
Since UnRocker lacks an attack detection mechanism, we activate both WARA’s and UnRocker’s recovery processes immediately after the attack to eliminate detection delay as a confounding factor.


UnRocker takes 35 to 56 ms to process each IMU sample and recover the gyroscope measurements. This far exceeds the HITL sampling interval (\(\Delta{t} = 4\) ms). Even without any acoustic attack, such a lengthy processing delay forces the drone into oscillation and eventually triggers a crash.
To verify this, we set the attack amplitude to zero (\(G_i = 0\)) and run UnRocker’s recovery. The drone still loses control quickly — the delay alone is fatal.



Impact of computational delay on online gyroscope recovery




UnRocker: Attack → Crash



UnRocker: No Attack → Delay-Induced Crash



WARA: Recovery >300 s



When an acoustic attack is active (\(G_i = 1.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 20\) Hz), UnRocker’s recovered measurements quickly deviate from the actual state, resulting in an immediate crash. In contrast, WARA’s recovered measurements stay consistent with the actual state, achieving effective recovery durations exceeding 300 seconds.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Comparison with UnRocker
text:
Offline Evaluation: Remote Gyroscope Attacks
To thoroughly compare the recovery quality of both approaches without the confounding effect of processing delay, we conduct an offline evaluation. We apply both WARA and UnRocker to the same compromised sensor data and compare the recovered signals against the ground truth.



Recovered gyroscope measurements under remote attack (\(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s)





Noise amplitude spectra under remote attack (\(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s)


Both WARA and UnRocker effectively suppress the acoustic injection attacks — the reduced sensor fluctuations and diminished attack signal peaks in the noise spectrum confirm this. However, UnRocker’s recovered measurements deviate from the drone’s actual state. When the actual pitch rate exceeds 1.0 rad/s, UnRocker returns near-zero output — the deep model distorts benign motion signals during its recovery process. The noise amplitude spectrum reveals new peaks in the 0–5 Hz band that were absent before recovery, confirming that UnRocker introduces low-frequency signal distortion.
WARA’s orthogonal wavelet packet transform preserves drone motion signals while efficiently removing induced signals. Because orthogonal WPD is an invertible linear transformation, it preserves the energy and details of the input — benign signal components pass through the recovery process intact.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Comparison with UnRocker
text:
HITL Supplementary Results
We further compare WARA and UnRocker’s offline recovery performance across additional attack scenarios. The figures below show results against touch-based gyroscope attacks on the ICM-42688P sensor, and simulated accelerometer attacks in HITL.

Gyroscope: Touch-Based Real-World Attack



Recovered gyroscope measurements (touch-based, \(G_i = 1.9\) rad/s)





Noise amplitude spectra of gyroscope recovery (touch-based, \(G_i = 1.9\) rad/s)




Accelerometer: HITL Simulated Attack



Recovered accelerometer measurements (HITL, \(G_i = 20.0\) m/s²)





Noise amplitude spectra of accelerometer recovery (HITL, \(G_i = 20.0\) m/s²)


In both simulated and real-world attacks, UnRocker’s recovery process consistently distorts drone motion signals. This is evidenced by the noise amplitude peaks in the spectra and the significant deviation in yaw rate and accelerometer measurements. The distortion effect is most pronounced with large-amplitude sensor signals, where UnRocker’s recovered output diverges sharply from the ground truth.


Noise Spectrum: WARA vs UnRocker vs Wiener



Noise amplitude spectrum comparison (\(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s)


UnRocker and the Wiener filter both introduce new noise peaks in the low-frequency band — distortions of the drone’s motion signal. WARA’s noise peak at lower frequencies, when present, has a considerably smaller amplitude. These results highlight WARA’s key advantage: effectively removing interference while preserving the integrity of benign signals.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
Comparison with Heuristic Filters
We compare WARA against three classical signal processing methods: Butterworth (2nd-order, 40 Hz cutoff, the PX4 default), Savitzky-Golay (polynomial order 6, window length 16, equivalent 40 Hz cutoff), and Wiener (window length 255).

Online SITL
Both Butterworth and Savitzky-Golay fail to remove the induced signal when the induced frequency falls below their 40 Hz cutoff. Reducing the cutoff further would capture low-frequency interference, but at the cost of increased control latency — directly compromising flight performance.
The Wiener filter effectively removes most induced signals, but its recovered measurements exhibit slower fluctuations and its noise spectrum reveals new peaks in the low-frequency band, causing the flight controller to overreact to attitude adjustments.



Recovered gyroscope data — SITL (\(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 20\) Hz)




HITL Results
The offline HITL results confirm the online findings. Butterworth and Savitzky-Golay cannot selectively filter out attack interference blended with motion signals. Wiener introduces low-frequency distortion — new peaks appear in the 0–5 Hz band after recovery. WARA isolates interference in the time-frequency domain, removing attack components while preserving motion signals intact.



Recovered gyroscope measurements — HITL (\(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s)





Noise amplitude spectra of gyroscope recovery — HITL (\(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s)





Recovered accelerometer measurements — HITL (\(G_i = 20.0\) m/s²)





Noise amplitude spectra of accelerometer recovery — HITL (\(G_i = 20.0\) m/s²)
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
Comparison with VIMU
VIMU is the SOTA physics-based attack resilience (PBAR) approach. It predicts system states using a nonlinear physical model and replaces compromised IMU data with these predictions. Unlike UnRocker, VIMU meets real-time constraints on our FC boards, enabling direct online HITL comparison.



Flight attitude in gyroscope recovery (HITL, \(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s)




VIMU: HITL → Crash



WARA: CUAV V5+ HITL Recovery



The drone equipped with VIMU quickly lost control and crashed shortly after the attack began. VIMU’s recovery duration in HITL is shorter than simulation results reported in prior work because we apply a significantly larger attack amplitude (4.0 vs. 0.6 rad/s). Additionally, sampling jitter affects VIMU’s physical model — the model predicts attitude changes per system control interval, but jitter disrupts the timing relationship between sensor samples and control updates.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
Comparison with SpecGuard
SpecGuard is the SOTA fault-tolerant control (FTC) approach. Unlike filter-based and PBAR solutions, SpecGuard does not directly recover compromised measurements. Instead, it employs a deep-RL robust controller that activates upon attack detection, aiming to keep the drone within predefined mission specifications (e.g., minimum flight altitude, position boundary). We deployed SpecGuard on Navio2@Raspberry Pi 3B+ for HITL simulation.



Flight altitude in gyroscope recovery (SpecGuard, HITL)


After the attack began, the drone quickly violated the minimum altitude specification and veered off the 10 m position boundary, resulting in a short recovery duration (< 10 seconds). SpecGuard’s performance degrades relative to reported simulation results because we apply the IMU attack to all gyroscope instances simultaneously — without reliable IMU measurements, the autopilot’s state estimation is disrupted, defeating the RL controller’s ability to maintain specifications.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
Comparison with UnRocker
UnRocker is the SOTA deep-learning-based denoising approach that uses an autoencoder to remove attack interference from a sliding window of IMU measurements. Both methods run on a Raspberry Pi 3B+, the only board in our test suite with sufficient memory (1 GB) to load UnRocker’s autoencoder model (1.43 MB). Since UnRocker lacks an attack detection mechanism, we activate both recovery processes immediately after the attack to eliminate detection delay as a confounding factor.

The Process Delay Problem
UnRocker takes 35 to 56 ms to process each IMU sample. This far exceeds the HITL sampling interval (\(\Delta{t} = 4\) ms). Even without any acoustic attack, such a lengthy processing delay forces the drone into oscillation and eventually triggers a crash. To verify, we set the attack amplitude to zero (\(G_i = 0\)) and run UnRocker’s recovery — the drone still loses control quickly.



Impact of computational delay on online gyroscope recovery




UnRocker: Attack → Crash



UnRocker: No Attack → Delay-Induced Crash



WARA: Recovery >300 s



When an acoustic attack is active (\(G_i = 1.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 20\) Hz), UnRocker’s recovered measurements quickly deviate from the actual state, resulting in an immediate crash. In contrast, WARA’s recovered measurements stay consistent with the actual state, achieving effective recovery durations exceeding 300 seconds.


Offline: Remote Gyroscope Attacks
To thoroughly compare recovery quality without the confounding effect of processing delay, we conduct an offline evaluation. Both methods are applied to the same compromised sensor data and compared against the ground truth.




Recovered gyroscope measurements (\(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s)





Noise amplitude spectra (\(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s)



Both methods effectively suppress the acoustic injection attacks — reduced sensor fluctuations and diminished attack signal peaks confirm this. However, UnRocker’s recovered measurements deviate from the drone’s actual state. When the actual pitch rate exceeds 1.0 rad/s, UnRocker returns near-zero output — the deep model distorts benign motion signals during recovery. The noise amplitude spectrum reveals new peaks in the 0–5 Hz band absent before recovery, confirming UnRocker introduces low-frequency signal distortion.
WARA’s orthogonal wavelet packet transform preserves drone motion signals while efficiently removing induced signals. Because orthogonal WPD is an invertible linear transformation, it preserves the energy and details of the input — benign signal components pass through recovery intact.


Offline: Touch-Based Real-World Attack
We further compare recovery against touch-based acoustic attacks on the ICM-42688P gyroscope sensor, where PZT discs are attached directly to the flight controller for stronger attack amplitudes.



Recovered gyroscope measurements (touch-based, \(G_i = 1.9\) rad/s)





Noise amplitude spectra of gyroscope recovery (touch-based, \(G_i = 1.9\) rad/s)




HITL: Accelerometer Attack



Recovered accelerometer measurements (HITL, \(G_i = 20.0\) m/s²)





Noise amplitude spectra of accelerometer recovery (HITL, \(G_i = 20.0\) m/s²)


In both real-world and simulated attacks, UnRocker’s recovery process consistently distorts drone motion signals — evidenced by noise amplitude peaks in the spectra and significant deviations in yaw rate and accelerometer measurements. The distortion is most pronounced with large-amplitude sensor signals.



Noise amplitude spectrum: WARA vs UnRocker vs Wiener (\(G_i = 4.0\) rad/s)


UnRocker and the Wiener filter both introduce new noise peaks in the low-frequency band — distortions of the drone’s motion signal. WARA’s noise peak at lower frequencies, when present, has a considerably smaller amplitude.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
To compare all approaches on a common metric, we use effective recovery duration: the elapsed time from the first detection alarm (\(t_{alarm}\)) until the drone’s estimated position deviates from the actual position by more than \(\epsilon = 3\) meters — indicating loss of stable flight. This metric reflects sustained operational safety under attack, not merely signal-level recovery quality. All results below are measured in HITL simulation on the same hardware for fair comparison.



Effective recovery duration across all baselines (HITL, \(G_i\) and \(f_i\) as labeled)


WARA achieves the longest effective recovery durations across all tested attack configurations. UnRocker’s high processing delay prevents timely recovery even when its signal-level denoising is effective. VIMU’s physical model degrades under strong attacks and sampling jitter. SpecGuard’s RL controller cannot compensate when all IMU instances are simultaneously compromised. The classical filters — Butterworth, Savitzky-Golay, and Wiener — either fail to remove low-frequency interference or introduce motion signal distortion that shortens recovery duration. The following sections examine each comparison in detail.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
text:
In conventional acoustic attacks, the oscillating induced signal contributes roughly the same amount to inertial measurements in both directions, limiting the accumulated error. To develop more capable attacks, prior work has introduced two signal modulation techniques:

Side-swing attack — The adversary increases the amplitude when the induced signal is in the target direction and decreases it otherwise, producing a net directional bias.
Switch attack — The attacker switches the acoustic frequency to keep the induced signal consistently in the same direction.

Both attacks induce low-frequency distortion in the target direction, allowing the adversary to not only disrupt sensor readings but also deliver implicit control and manipulate drone motion. These are substantially more dangerous than unmodulated acoustic injections.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
text:
In conventional acoustic attacks, the oscillating induced signal contributes roughly the same amount to inertial measurements in both directions, limiting the accumulated error. To develop more capable attacks, prior work has introduced two signal modulation techniques:

Side-swing attack — The adversary increases the amplitude when the induced signal is in the target direction and decreases it otherwise, producing a net directional bias.
Switch attack — The attacker switches the acoustic frequency to keep the induced signal consistently in the same direction.

Both attacks induce low-frequency distortion in the target direction, allowing the adversary to not only disrupt sensor readings but also deliver implicit control and manipulate drone motion. These are substantially more dangerous than unmodulated acoustic injections.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
text:
Evaluation Setup
We evaluate WARA’s recovery performance against advanced attacks in SITL simulation. The primary IMU is targeted with either a side-swing or switch attack at an induced frequency \(f_i = 1\) Hz. The reserved IMUs are targeted with regular simulated attacks at \(f_i = 20\) Hz. All attacks use an amplitude of \(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s. WARA operates in multi-IMU mode to leverage the redundant IMUs for low-frequency interference removal.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
text:
WARA Recovery Results
The advanced attacks inject strong low-frequency interference into the primary IMU measurements, causing significant deviations from the ground truth. WARA’s multi-IMU mode identifies the affected LF wavelet packets and replaces them with benign coefficients from a redundant — but also compromised — IMU instance.




Recovered gyroscope measurements (SITL, \(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 1\) Hz)





Noise amplitude spectra (SITL, \(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 1\) Hz)



The recovered measurements show substantially reduced interference in the attacked frequency bands, enabling the drone to maintain stability and complete its mission.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
section: Comparison with UnRocker and Single-IMU WARA
text:
Comparison with UnRocker and Single-IMU WARA
In contrast, both UnRocker and WARA’s single-IMU mode are ineffective against these advanced attacks:




Recovered gyroscope measurements: WARA vs UnRocker vs Single-IMU WARA (SITL, \(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 1\) Hz)





Noise amplitude spectra comparison (SITL, \(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s, \(f_i = 1\) Hz)



Their recovered results deviate significantly from the ground truth, and the noise peaks in the 0–5 Hz band confirm that LF interference persists after their recovery processes. These results demonstrate that WARA’s multi-IMU mode is essential for removing low-frequency attack interference — single-IMU recovery and deep-learning denoising both fail when the attack energy is concentrated within the motion signal band.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
text:
Limitations
We investigated WARA’s boundaries by varying attack configurations. Increasing \(G_i\) on the primary IMU above 2.0 rad/s destabilizes the flight attitude, triggering a no position fail-safe due to a false alarm in the GNSS sensor. Simultaneously attacking all IMUs with side-swing or switch attacks results in a crash — these attacks all produce LF interference, creating overlapping distortion within the narrow motion signal band (0–5 Hz). However, simultaneously crafting multiple independent modulated signals into this band remains challenging, as discussed in the paper.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Advanced Attack Waveforms
text:
Impact on Closed-Loop Control
To directly quantify recovery quality at the control level, we measure the mean attitude error across roll, pitch, and yaw axes. We conducted 10 SITL flights per configuration. The attitude error is the absolute deviation between the estimated Euler angles and the ground-truth states.



Mean attitude error under Switch Attack (\(G_i = 2.0\) rad/s)


Under attack-free conditions, WARA maintains a performance profile nearly identical to the vanilla PX4 autopilot, with errors consistently below 0.02 rad — confirming WARA introduces no functional degradation during routine operations.
Under the Switch Attack, the vanilla autopilot’s attitude error surges to 0.5–1.0 rad, a magnitude that triggers an immediate crash. In contrast, WARA suppresses the error to 0.05–0.10 rad across all axes. Although this remains higher than the benign state, it stays well within the autopilot’s compensation envelope — the drone maintains flight attitude and completes its mission.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV

title: Recovery Effectiveness
text:
Effective Recovery Duration
To compare all approaches on a common metric, we use effective recovery duration: the elapsed time from the first detection alarm (\(t_{alarm}\)) until the drone’s estimated position deviates from the actual position by more than \(\epsilon = 3\) meters — indicating loss of stable flight. This metric reflects sustained operational safety under attack, not merely signal-level recovery quality.
All results below are measured in HITL simulation on the same hardware for fair comparison.



Effective recovery duration across all baselines (HITL, \(G_i\) and \(f_i\) as labeled)


WARA achieves the longest effective recovery durations across all tested attack configurations. UnRocker’s high processing delay prevents timely recovery even when its signal-level denoising is effective. VIMU’s physical model degrades under strong attacks and sampling jitter. SpecGuard’s RL controller cannot compensate when all IMU instances are simultaneously compromised. The classical filters — Butterworth, Savitzky-Golay, and Wiener — either fail to remove low-frequency interference or introduce motion signal distortion that shortens recovery duration.
crumbs: WARA: Wavelet-based Realtime Recovery against Acoustic Injection Attacks on UAV
